title: Sickle cell disease in Africa: trial-by-trial analysis of trial share and disease burden
primary_estimand: Condition Colonialism Index (CCI) for SCD trials in Africa
certainty: high (registry data, complete enumeration within ClinicalTrials.gov)
body: What is the relationship between Africa's sickle cell disease burden and its share of global clinical trials for this condition? We queried ClinicalTrials.gov for interventional sickle cell disease studies with African sites and classified each trial by sponsor, design origin, termination status, and likely local relevance. Among roughly five thousand global sickle cell trial records through March 2026, we identified about eighty unique trials with African locations. Africa carries 75% of the global sickle cell burden yet hosts under 2% of registered trials, yielding a Condition Colonialism Index of 47.7. Ten trials were terminated by pharmaceutical sponsors following decisions made outside Africa, and about thirty-five were multinational mega-trials evaluating drugs priced far above local affordability thresholds. Only about fifteen to twenty trials appeared to be genuinely designed around African clinical priorities, most commonly hydroxyurea adherence and dosing studies. The analysis is limited to one registry and will miss studies listed only on national or WHO platforms.
ai_transparency: LLM assistance was used in early drafting. The final text was rewritten by the author, who takes full responsibility for the content.

checks.value: CCI 47x is most extreme mismatch for any major disease globally
single registry, cannot capture national/WHO platform registrations
